hook.text: VisiCalc wasn't just software. It was the reason millions of people bought a personal computer for the first time.

buildup.text: Dan Bricklin was a Harvard MBA student tired of recalculating financial models by hand on paper ledger sheets.

discovery.text: He built VisiCalc for the Apple II in 1979. It turned a hobbyist toy into a serious business tool overnight.

twist.text: Apple II sales exploded — not because of the hardware, but because accountants needed VisiCalc. Software drove hardware sales.

climax.text: VisiCalc proved a lasting product truth: the killer app matters more than the platform it runs on.

punchline.text: One app turned a hobby computer into a business necessity.

quiz.question: Why was VisiCalc significant in product history?
options: It proved that a killer app can drive platform adoption
It was the most expensive software of its era
It was built by Apple's engineering team
